questions.questionText: Intussusception in children typically occurs at:
questions.optionB: 6-9 months (ileocolic type most common — colicky pain, red currant jelly stools, sausage-shaped mass)
questions.explanation: Intussusception: telescoping of proximal bowel into distal segment. Peak: 6-9 months (3 months - 3 years). Most common: ileocolic (ileum invaginates into colon). Triad: colicky abdominal pain (episodic), red currant jelly stools (blood + mucus — late sign), sausage-shaped mass (RUQ). Dance sign: emptiness in RIF. USG: target/doughnut sign (transverse), pseudokidney sign (longitudinal). Treatment: non-operative reduction — pneumatic (air) or hydrostatic (saline) enema under fluoroscopy/USG (<24h, no peritonitis). Surgery if failed/perforation. Reference: OP Ghai — Intussusception.

questions.optionB: Absence of ganglion cells (aganglionosis) in the myenteric and submucosal plexuses of distal bowel — results in functional obstruction
questions.explanation: Hirschsprung disease: congenital aganglionosis — failure of neural crest cell migration → absent ganglion cells in distal bowel (rectosigmoid in 75%). Functional obstruction (narrowed aganglionic segment, dilated proximal normal bowel). Presentation: delayed passage of meconium (>24-48h after birth), abdominal distension, bilious vomiting, chronic constipation. Diagnosis: rectal suction biopsy (gold standard — absent ganglion cells, hypertrophied nerve trunks, increased AChE). Barium enema: transition zone (narrow distal→dilated proximal). Treatment: surgical — pull-through procedure (Swenson/Duhamel/Soave). Reference: OP Ghai — Hirschsprung.
